name: Flee
description: An AI survival behavior where an agent chooses a safer destination away from a threat or hazardous state.
design_notes: Flee behavior is more than moving opposite a target. It needs threat_score, safe_position candidates, navmesh_query validation, commitment_min_ms, hysteresis_margin, and arrival rules so agents do not oscillate or run into worse danger.
design_purpose: Make agents appear self-preserving when outmatched, low on health, or exposed.
Create dynamic combat spacing and opportunities for pursuit, mercy, or crowd control.
Move noncombatants, companions, or wildlife away from hazards without scripted paths.
player_fantasy: The player feels their pressure matters when enemies, civilians, or creatures react by escaping danger.

parameters.name: threat_score_threshold
parameters.description: Score above which the agent considers entering flee state.

parameters.name: safe_destination_sample_count
parameters.description: Number of candidate positions evaluated before choosing a flee destination.

parameters.name: minimum_threat_distance
parameters.description: Desired separation from the active threat after flee movement.

parameters.name: commitment_min_ms
parameters.description: Minimum flee commitment window before reevaluating another state.

parameters.name: hysteresis_margin
parameters.description: Score buffer that prevents rapid switching between flee, chase, and idle states.

edge_cases: All sampled safe destinations are unreachable, closer to another threat, or outside allowed combat bounds.
Several agents choose the same cover or exit and block each other.
The threat disappears during commitment_min_ms, requiring a rule for continue, search, or resume previous state.
A fleeing agent reaches a locked door, cliff edge, or navmesh boundary and needs a fallback behavior.
common_bugs: Agents flee directly away from target_id and run into walls because no navmesh_query evaluates the path.
Threat scores drop below threshold for one frame and cancel flee, creating visible jitter.
Animation root motion continues after AI state changes, moving the agent back toward danger.
Network clients predict different safe destinations because candidate ordering is not deterministic.
balancing_notes: Frequent flee behavior can make combat feel evasive and slow; reserve it for readable health, morale, or fear states.
Flee destinations should create tactical repositioning, not guaranteed invulnerability or unreachable enemies.
Use commitment and hysteresis to make behavior legible rather than twitchy.
accessibility_notes: Use clear posture, icon, bark, or movement cues so players understand the agent is fleeing rather than bugging out.
Avoid making required targets flee into visually dense spaces without tracking aids or readable silhouettes.

implementation_notes.unity: Sample candidate points on the NavMesh, score them by threat distance and path validity, store chosen safe_position and ai_state_id, and draw debug rays for rejected candidates.
implementation_notes.godot: Use NavigationServer or NavigationAgent queries for flee candidates and keep threat_score plus commitment timer in the state machine to prevent oscillation.
implementation_notes.unreal: Use EQS or navigation queries to score flee destinations, store ThreatActor and SafeLocation in Blackboard, and apply StateTree/Behavior Tree decorators for hysteresis.
implementation_notes.web: Evaluate deterministic candidate positions from a seeded order, run grid/nav queries, and keep flee commitment separate from render animation so replay/debug traces match.

example_games.title: The Sims
example_games.note: Uses panic-like routing away from hazards in simulation scenarios.

example_games.title: Half-Life
example_games.note: Uses enemy and NPC retreat behaviors in combat encounters.
